bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Wears No. 20
  • Parents are Lee and Barbie Arioto
  • Has one brother, Tim
  • Has three dogs: Daisy, Bella and Chuy
  • Dream job is a sports broadcaster
  • Played professionally in the Japan Softball League
  • Favorite activities include bowling, reading, learning, and softball
Career Highlights
  • 2019 Pan American Games Gold Medalist; Hit .176 (3-for-17) with 2 RBI and 4 runs scored
  • 2018 USA Softball International Cup; Hit .400 (6-for-15) with five HR, 13 RBI, 10 runs scored and a 1.467 slugging percentage
  • 2017 Pan American Championship Gold Medalist; Hit .357 with 10 runs scored, 18 RBI and eight walks
  • 2015 Pan American Games Silver Medalist; Hit .364 with one HR, eight RBI and a 1.091 slugging percentage
  • 2011 Pan American Games Gold Medalist; Hit .385 with four RBI and seven runs scored
  • Four time World Cup of Softball Gold Medalist
Olympic Experience
  • 1-time Olympian; 1-time Olympic medalist (1 silver)
    • Olympic Games Tokyo 2020, silver (Team - Women)
World Championship Experience
  • Most recent: 2018 – gold (Team - Women)
  • Years of participation: Team - Women 2012, 2014, 2016, 2018
  • Medals: 4 (2 gold, 2 silver)
    • Gold – 2018 (Team - Women); 2016 (Team - Women)
    • Silver – 2014 (Team - Women); 2012 (Team - Women)
More

COLLEGIATE HONORS

2012: Easton Softball All-American First Team ...NFCA All-American First Team ...Pac-12 All-American First Team ...Pac-12 Player of the Year ...Named to the USA Softball Top-50, Top-25, Top-10, and Top-3 watch list for Player of the Year. Received the Tom Hansen Conference Medal and the Lowe's Senior Class award.

2011: Red Shirt due to injury  

2010: Named to the Louisville Slugger/NFCA All-American First Team ...Also placed on the All-Pac-10 first team ...Named to the USA Softball Top-50, Top-25 and Top-10 watch lists for Player of the Year

2009: Third-team NFCA All-American ...Second-team Easton All-American ...First-team All-Pac-10 and first team All-Pacific Region selection

2008: All-Pac-10 second-team ...NFCA All-Pacific Region second-team selection ...Named to the Fresno All-Regional team and recipient of the Donna Terry Award (Team MVP)

bio.education: Falcon High School (Peyton, Colo.) ’93 University of Northern Colorado, B.A. Sociology; Sam Houston State University ‘00, M.A. Criminal Justice Administration
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Daughter of Kenneth and Linda Armbruster
  • Has one sister, Stacy
  • Married to U.S. teammate, Asya Miller
  • Has one son, Ryder
  • Began losing her eyesight at 14 years old after experiencing pain in both eyes
  • Has no central vision but was left with some usable peripheral vision out of her left eye
  • Hobbies include skiing, rock climbing, cycling, reading and playing with her son
  • Began playing goalball after a teacher at a local school for the blind read a newspaper article on her and encouraged her to try the sport
  • Has traveled to 45 states in the United States
  • Shaken hands with three U.S. presidents
  • Inducted into the Colorado Springs Sports Hall of Fame, State of Colorado Sports Hall of Fame and the Colorado Women’s Sports Hall of Fame.
Paralympic Experience
  • Seven-time Paralympian (1992, 1996, 2000, 2004, 2008, 2012, 2016); Four-time Paralympic medalist (1 gold, 1 silver, 2 bronze)
  • Rio 2016 Paralympic Games, bronze
  • Beijing 2008 Paralympic Games, gold
  • Athens 2004 Paralympic Games, silver
  • Sydney 2000 Paralympic Games, bronze
World Championship Experience
  • Most recent: 2014 – gold
  • Years of Participation: 2014, 2010, 2006, 2002, 1998
  • Medals: 5 (2 gold, 1 silver, 2 bronze)
  • Gold – 2014; 2002
  • Silver – 2010
  • Bronze – 2006; 1998

bio.education: Kubasaki High School (Okinawa, Japan) ‘91 University of Idaho '95, Exercise physiology
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Daughter of William and Cora Armstrong
  • Has one brother, Eric
  • Born into a military family, spending her early childhood in Tennessee and California before attending high school in Japan
  • Married Joe Savola in 2007
  • Has one son, Lucas
  • Previously served as a project manager for an advertising agency
  • Prior to her cycling career, she was a world-class triathlete and competed in the 1999 Ironman World Championships in Hawaii
  • After being diagnosed with osteoarthritis of the hips she was forced away from high-impact activities, such as running
  • Co-owner of K-Edge Cycling Solutions, a component manufacturer that received its start designing and manufacturing chain catchers for her Olympic bikes
  • Active with the YMCA, U.S. Anti-Doping Agency and community-building activities
  • Enjoys being a mom, mountain biking, cooking and Nordic skiing
  • Announced her return to professional cycling in 2015.
Career Highlights
  • U-15 World Cup, Sinaloa, 2014 (Team - Men)
Olympic Experience
  • 4-time Olympian; 3-time Olympic medalist (3 gold)
    • Olympic Games Rio de Janeiro 2016, (Road race - Women), gold (Individual time trial - Women)
    • Olympic Games London 2012, gold (Individual time trial - Women), 35th (Road race - Women)
    • Olympic Games Beijing 2008, gold (Individual time trial - Women), 25th (Road race - Women)
    • Olympic Games Athens 2004, 8th (Road race - Women)
World Championship Experience
  • Most recent: 2016 – 5th (Team time trial - Women)
  • Years of participation: Team time trial - Women 2016; Individual time trial - Women 2003, 2005, 2006, 2007, 2008, 2009, 2015; Road race - Women 2003, 2004, 2005, 2006, 2007, 2008, 2009; 3km Individual Pursuit - Women 2006
  • Medals: 4 (2 gold, 1 silver, 1 bronze)
    • Gold – 2009 (Individual time trial - Women); 2006 (Individual time trial - Women)
    • Silver – 2007 (Individual time trial - Women)
    • Bronze – 2005 (Individual time trial - Women)

bio.education: Knightdale High School (Knightdale, N.C.) ‘08 University of Oklahoma ‘10, Sociology
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Son of Jeremy and Yolanda Baker
  • Has three sisters, Jada Baker, Kiara Turner and Shaniqua Harris
  • Holds high school program records in both the 110- and 300-meter hurdles, and competed in the high jump at Knightdale High School
  • Began hurdling in high school and was recruited to play basketball in college
  • Did not plan on attending college, but received an offer from Bethune-Cookman University
  • Transferred to the University of Oklahoma after his sophomore year
  • Aspires to pursue a career in social work and help children in need.
Olympic Experience
  • 1-time Olympian
    • Olympic Games Rio de Janeiro 2016, 8th (110m Hurdles - Men)
World Championship Experience
  • Most recent: 2015 – (110m Hurdles - Men)
  • Years of participation: 110m Hurdles - Men 2015
  • Top finish: – 2015 (110m Hurdles - Men)

Event/Discipline: Canoe Sprint, K1-500m
Height: 5’4”
Birthdate: 2/6/91
Hometown: Hoschton, Ga.
Current residence: Hoschton, Ga.
College/school: Quantum University
Club/team: Lanier Canoe & Kayak Club

Career highlights:
  • 2009 U.S. Junior World Championship Team Member (K-4)
  • 2010 U.S. Dragon Boat Team Member
  • 2011 U.S. Under-23 Team Member

Recent results:
  • Won the U-23 K1 500m at USA National Team Trials
  • Won two silver medals at the International Ruhrregatta in Bochum Germany for Senior Women’s K4 200m & 500m in 2012.
  • Won a bronze medal for Senior women’s K1 1000m Silver medal for Senior Women’s K1 200m and a Silver medal for Senior K4 200m at the Magdeburger Kanuregatta in Magdeburg Germany 2012. 
  • Won the Senior women’s 500m K4 at US Nationals in 2012 with a Bronze in Senior Women’s K2 500m &1000m.
Nickname: Flossy May
Job: Masters kayak coach
Hobbies: SUP boarding, Photography
Favorite quote: The obstacle in front of you is never greater than the power within you.

How’d you get started? Through the middle school program my club offers. My mother signed me up for the spring season and I was hooked the first day. I have been doing it ever sense.
Who’s your hero? My mother, she’s just such a strong women, I really look up to her, she’s the major reason I do what I do and have gotten this far. She has taught me to always give my best, work hard and to never do anything half way. She raised me to be confident and never give up that anything is attainable. For that I will always thank her.
Trivia: I absolutely love animals, I’m super outdoorsy and I guess you could say a bit of a tom boy.

Career Highlights
  • World Junior & Under 23 Championships, Welland, 2013 (K1 500m - Women)
  • World Junior Championships, Moscow, 2009 (K4 500m - Women)

bio.education: University of Texas at Arlington, New York University
bio.biography:
Quick Facts

  • Daughter of Theodore Aslakson and Becky Miller

  • Was involved in a car crash at the age of five that caused her to be paralyzed from the waist down

  • Discovered basketball at the age of 13 at the Courage Center in Minneapolis, Minnesota after seeing the basketball team practice while she was at an archery lesson
  • Played basketball at Jordan High School before attending the University of Texas at Arlington where she played for the Lady Movin’ Mavs
  • Transferred to New York University to study dramatic writing
  • Currently the head coach of the women's wheelchair basketball team at the University of Arizona
  • Hobbies include writing, art, film, spirituality and traveling
Paralympic Experience
  • 2-time Paralympian; 2-time Paralympic medalist (1 silver, 1 bronze)
    • Paralympic Games Paris 2024, silver (Team - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Tokyo 2020, bronze (Team - Women)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2023 – bronze (Team - Women)
  • Years of participation: Team - Women 2018, 2023
  • Medals: 1 (1 bronze)
    • Bronze – 2023 (Team - Women)

Quick Facts
  • Daughter of TJ and Jennifer Aspden
  • Has one brother, Michael, and one sister, Sarah
  • Aspden began swimming competitively at the age of eight with the YMCA of the Triangle Area (YOTA) in Raleigh, North Carolina
  • She was born with congenital hip disarticulation and has no left leg
  • While she enjoyed participating in a variety of sports, swimming came naturally to her at an early age
  • She made her first national team at the age of 13 in 2014 by a margin of .01 second
  • She has shined since then, representing Team USA at multiple Pan-Pacific and world championships, as well as other international competitions
  • Was the youngest Team USA swimmer to earn a medal at either the Olympic Games and Paralympic Games in Rio
  • Hobbies include writing, art, reading and photography
  • She collects magnets from all of the destinations where she competes
  • Has a golden retriever named Max
  • Favorite movies are The Avengers and any Marvel Comic movies
  • Her favorite kind of music is classic rock.
Paralympic Experience
  • 3-time Paralympian; 4-time Paralympic medalist (2 gold, 2 bronze)
    • Paralympic Games Paris 2024, 6th (100m Backstroke S9 - Women, 4 x 100m Medley Relay 34 Points - Mixed), 11th (100m Freestyle S9 - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Tokyo 2020, (4 x 100m Freestyle Relay 34 Points - Women), gold (100m Backstroke S9 - Women, 4 x 100m Medley Relay 34 Points - Women), 10th (100m Breaststroke SB8 - Women), 11th (100m Freestyle S9 - Women, 200m Individual Medley SM9 - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Rio de Janeiro 2016, bronze (100m Backstroke S9 - Women, 4 x 100m Medley Relay 34 Points - Women), 9th (400m Freestyle S9 - Women), 11th (50m Freestyle S9 - Women), 15th (100m Freestyle S9 - Women), 17th (200m Individual Medley SM9 - Women)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2023 – 5th (4 x 100m Medley Relay 34 Points - Mixed), 6th (100m Backstroke S9 - Women), 9th (100m Freestyle S9 - Women), 12th (200m Individual Medley SM9 - Women, 50m Freestyle S9 - Women)
  • Years of participation: 100m Backstroke S9 - Women 2015, 2017, 2022, 2023; 100m Freestyle S9 - Women 2015, 2017, 2022, 2023; 200m Individual Medley SM9 - Women 2015, 2022, 2023; 4 x 100m Medley Relay 34 Points - Mixed 2022, 2023; 50m Freestyle S9 - Women 2015, 2017, 2022, 2023; 4 x 100m Freestyle Relay 34 Points - Women 2015, 2017; 4 x 100m Medley Relay 34 Points - Women 2015, 2017; 400m Freestyle S9 - Women 2015, 2017
  • Medals: 5 (2 gold, 3 silver)
    • Gold – 2017 (4 x 100m Freestyle Relay 34 Points - Women, 4 x 100m Medley Relay 34 Points - Women)
    • Silver – 2022 (100m Backstroke S9 - Women); 2017 (100m Backstroke S9 - Women); 2015 (4 x 100m Freestyle Relay 34 Points - Women)

Quick Facts
  • Daughter of Byron and LaShonda Atkins
  • Has one older sister, Jessica, and one younger brother, Byron Jr
  • Participated in the U.S. Olympic Committee’s Finding Leaders Among Minorities Everywhere (FLAME) program during the summer of 2016.
Career Highlights
  • WNBA All-Defensive second team: 2018, 2019, 2020
  • WNBA All-Rookie Team: 2018
  • 2014 FIBA Americas U18 Championship (Gold)
Olympic Experience
  • 1-time Olympian; 1-time Olympic medalist (1 gold)
    • Olympic Games Tokyo 2020, gold (Team - Women)

bio.education: Central Valley High School (Spokane Valley, Wash.) ‘21
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Son of Morgen and Jeffrey
  • Has a sister, Lillian
  • Adopted from China
  • Born with a birth defect called proximal femoral focal discrepancy with minor spina bifida
  • Underwent a amputation of his left foot, knee fusion, and bone graft connecting his under-developed left femur to his knee, tibia and fibula
  • Introduced to adaptive sports through ParaSport Spokane, beginning with wheelchair basketball and transitioning to track
  • Enjoys riding his bike, playing Xbox, collecting sunglasses, curating digital music playlists, handing out with friends, watching movies, playing school sports and attending school activities
  • Learned to scuba dive in the Grand Caymans through a program called Stay Focused
  • Plays the guitar, clarinet and marimba
  • Can code in Javascript.
Career Highlights
  • ParaPan American Games, Lima, 2019 (100m T54 - Men, 1500m T54 - Men, 400m T54 - Men)

Career Highlights
  • World Cup, 2019 (Team - Men)
  • Pan American Games, Toronto, 2015 (Team - Men)
Quick Facts
  • Son of Cherie and Andrew Augspurger
  • Serves as a youth development mentors coach at Play Rugby USA
